summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Application by the de facto husband and his parents for costs of the property division proceedings – Where the evidence of financial circumstances is scant – Parties’ conduct of the proceedings – Where the de facto wife was not wholly unsuccessful – Where the de facto wife bettered the offer of compromise in the final orders – No costs order justified – Application dismissed – Applicants to pay the costs of the respondent in a fixed sum.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE J:

On 14 August 2025 I made the following orders in property settlement proceedings between the de facto wife, Ms Jingyi (“the respondent”), and the de facto husband, Mr Chao (“the first applicant”):
1. All previous orders are discharged.
The liability arising under the loan agreement between the [respondent] and the first [applicant] dated 29 March 2021 is set aside.
Except as otherwise provided for by these orders, as against the first[applicant], the [respondent] shall be entitled to retain as the sole beneficial owner her bank savings, superannuation entitlements, and all other assets and financial resources of whatsoever nature and kind presently in her possession, control, or name.
Except as otherwise provided for by these orders, as against the [respondent], the first [applicant] shall be entitled to retain as the sole beneficial owner his bank savings, superannuation entitlements, and all other assets and financial resources of whatsoever nature and kind presently in his possession, control, or name.
Pursuant to s 106A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), in the event either party refuses or neglects to execute any deed or instrument necessary to give effect to these orders then the registrar of a court of competent jurisdiction in relation to the Act is appointed to execute such deed or instrument in the name of the defaulting party and do all acts and things necessary to give validity and operation to the deed or instrument.

6. All outstanding applications are otherwise dismissed.
I incorporate the reasons I gave into this judgment (Jingyi & Chao (No 9) [2025] FedCFamC1F 544).
The second and third applicants are the first applicant’s parents (together, “the applicants”). The applicants seek an order that the respondent pay their costs of the entirety of the proceedings or, alternatively, part of them.
The respondent commenced the proceedings on 18 May 2021 seeking parenting orders. On 21 June 2021 the first applicant filed a Response seeking both parenting and property orders.
At about the same time, the respondent took proceedings against the first applicant in the Supreme Court of New South Wales seeking repayment of a loan in the sum of $650,000. Those proceedings were stayed to await the outcome of this matter.
The parenting aspect was resolved by consent orders made on 25 February 2025.
Meanwhile the property proceedings expanded with the inclusion of the second and third applicants and a further respondent, C Pty Ltd (a company owned and controlled by the applicants). I shall return to the nature of the claims involving them shortly.
Costs in this matter are to be determined pursuant to s 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). The hearing commenced prior to the repeal of that section so it continues to apply (cl 15 of Sch 4 of the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth). See generally Navickas & Fried (No 2) (2025) FLC 94-248).
Under that section, parties to family law proceedings are to bear his or her own costs unless, in all of the circumstances, the court considers a different order is just. In considering such an order, the Court must take into account the matters set out in s 117(2A).
The applicants relied upon the following matters.

The financial circumstances of the parties

The evidence offered by the applicants was scant and came by way of an affidavit of their solicitor. That evidence was:
17. Based on the financial statements filed by both parties:
the [respondent] has disclosed ownership of two properties with a total value of $E5,443,633.63;
the [first applicant] has disclosed weekly income of $500 and Centrelink benefits of $189.38;
the second applicant and the third applicant (“the Parents”) are both unemployed and have no income;
the Parents have disclosed ownership of the property at [YY Street], Suburb Q NSW (“the [Suburb Q] Property”). However, the property was the subject of freezing orders during the proceedings and is presently listed for sale.

...
The proceedings have cost the applicants significant amounts of their cash assets. I set out below a comparison of the applicants’ cash assets at the commencement of proceedings and present date:
A. Position as at 25 January 2021:
the Parents had cash available in the loan account for the [Suburb Q] Property in the sum of $3, 016,330.50 (account no. [...94]).
A copy of the bank statement is annexed hereto and marked “L5” at pages 59 to 61.
B. Position as at 11 July 2025:
the first [applicant’s] bank accounts disclosed the following balances:
$300.00 (account no. [...49]);
$1,300.00 (account no. [...39]);
$1,622.14 (account no. [...90]);
Total: $3,222.14
A copy of the first [applicant’s] bank statements is annexed hereto and marked “L6” at pages 62 to 85.
the Parents’ bank account showed a balance of $1,981.31 (account no. [...77]). A copy of the Parents’ bank statements is annexed hereto and marked “L7” at pages 86 to 88.
as at 13 June 2025, the mortgagee alleged that the Parents’ loan account for the [Suburb Q] Property was in debit in the sum of $19,087,270.93. A copy of the loan account statement is annexed hereto and marked “L8” at pages 89 to 99.
(Affidavit of [Ms AD] filed 11 September 2025)
There is no evidence linking the financial position outlined above with the proceedings. No explanation was given as to where the money had gone. No current value of the Suburb Q property was provided. It is difficult, therefore, to identify the financial circumstances of the parents.
The respondent, at present, is in a more favourable position financially than the first applicant.

The conduct of the parties in the proceedings

The applicants submitted that the respondent “has drawn out the proceedings longer than should have occurred due to the many interim applications and thereby incurred significant expense for the applicants” (applicants’ written submissions filed 18 November 2025, paragraph 30).
The respondent had filed 18 Applications in a Proceeding, 13 of which were said to seek substantive or interim orders.
Neither the evidence nor the submissions went into greater detail than this. There was no discussion as to whether the applications were successful or not, or justifiable or not. Simply, the sheer number was relied upon.
The submission entirely loses its force when it is noted that the first applicant filed 14 interim applications himself.

Whether any party was wholly unsuccessful

The applicants submitted that the respondent had been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings.
There were a number of issues to be determined in the proceedings.
The respondent asserted that the first applicant owed her $650,000 plus interest. The first applicant denied this. The respondent succeeded.
The first applicant sought the transfer of 75 per cent of the respondent’s property to him. The respondent opposed any alteration of property interests. The only alteration to those interests was to cancel the loan.
Neither party could be said to be wholly unsuccessful.
The respondent unsuccessfully claimed that the first applicant had a beneficial interest in his parents’ property at Suburb Q.
The respondent unsuccessfully contended that the first applicant held cryptocurrency. The first applicant’s evidence on this topic was difficult to accept but, even so, a current holding could not be identified. It was, however, a reasonable claim to pursue.
The first applicant unsuccessfully sought a number of add backs.
The parents and C Pty Ltd each brought a claim against the respondent pursuant to usual undertakings as to damages given by her when obtaining interim injunctions. The claims failed.
I do not see how it is possible for it to be asserted that the respondent was wholly unsuccessful.

An offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle them and the terms of any such offer

On 23 February 2025, the solicitors for the applicants sent three letters from each of their clients containing an offer to settle the proceedings to the respondent (Exhibit “L2” to the affidavit of Ms AD filed 11 September 2025).
The letter on behalf of the parents proposed that the respondent’s claim against them be dismissed. It made no mention of the claims made against the respondent by them.
The letter on behalf of the first applicant proposed that the parties each retain the property and superannuation held by them and that the respondent’s claim against him, including the one relating to the loan, be dismissed.
The letter on behalf of C Pty Ltd provided for the respondent to pay $1.7 million within 28 days and that all proceedings between them be dismissed. It added “[The applicants] consent to the same that their points of claim against [the respondent] in these proceedings is to be dismissed”.
Reading these offers together, the effect is that the first applicant would be released from the loan and the respondent would pay C Pty Ltd $1.7 million and the proceedings would otherwise be dismissed. So understood, the respondent bettered this offer by $1.7 million by running her case.
Even if the offers are treated separately, which is difficult to do given the addendum to the last offer that I have just noted, I do not think that it can be said that the respondent acted unreasonably in refusing them. As explained in the substantive reasons, there was an inherent difficulty in the proceedings once the loan to the first applicant was established. The claims made by the respondent otherwise were unsuccessful but reasonably brought. The offer by C Pty Ltd, which was part of the package, was rightly rejected – its claim failed.
Taking these matters into account, I am quite unpersuaded that the default position provided for in s 117 should be altered.

RESPONDENT’S COSTS

The respondent sought her costs of the costs application which has been entirely unsuccessful. Some of the submissions made in support of it were quite untenable. Taking that into account with all the above matters, I am satisfied that, in all of the circumstances, a costs order is just.
The respondent identified the costs she is seeking as $22,000 (Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 28 October 2025). There was no attempt to justify that sum which seems excessive. It is hardly in anyone’s interests, let alone hers or the Court’s for there to be an assessment involving further costs and expenses. Doing the best I can, there will be an order that the applicants, jointly and severally, pay the respondent’s costs fixed in the sum of $2,500 within 28 days.
